speaker: interviewee
text: These two pictures were taken by my brother as he was visiting our hometown, Bendin, in 1974. The house is no longer there anymore. It was an old house to begin with. The whole area has been cleared up. It's absolutely hard to remember where any of the houses were standing. Whereas when we remember it, it was a densely populated neighborhood teeming with people. And now all this here is nothing but open spaces, and we could not find the spot where our house was. We were just stood-- stood-- standing there. So I had to imagine, because all the landmarks were nearby were still there. But we could not figure out where our house is. It's nothing but open fields. This is the house that looked like in 1974 when he was last there. It's no more. It's gone. The next picture that I have retrieved from relatives in Israel and in France who survived the war is this picture here-- my mother. This is before my sister was born. My mother-- so it had to be before 1926. This picture was taken 1920-- my mother and my father and myself and my brother. Then the next one is in 1937. This picture is in 1937, my brother and myself. And also a picture of my parents and my sister in 1937-- my mother, my father, and my sister. In 1937, she was 11 years old. She was 16 years old when she disappeared in Auschwitz. And this is a picture in the ghetto, 1941-- the three of us, the three kids. And this is the last picture we know of together. And there's one more. There's a family picture-- a family picture of my parents and their family in 1938. I vividly remember when this was taken. This is a negative from a negative, but it's my father and my mother and all uncles and aunts in one of our uncle's houses, which was a very close-knit family. This is not the whole family. It was just one evening visiting. And we all cherish so much, all our cousins that survived-- one in Israel-- two in Israel and two in-- one in Paris now. We all cherish this picture because we feel so much-- we can identify so much with it. This was 1938. And this is a picture of our family here in 1993. 1993. My brother has got five grandchildren and his daughter-in-law and my wife and his wife and so forth. This is the most recent picture. Of course we have other pictures, but I just couldn't put my hands on them. And that is it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait-- wait a second. Wait a second. Wait one second. I brought-- I brought down something here, and where the heck did I put it?

speaker: interviewer
text: There's something in the corner. Is that what you're looking for, the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it. OK. That's it. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell me when-- when to go. Right after the war when we were in the convalescent homes, we were given all kind of tools and try to give us some kind of things to be busy with. And this is one of my works I've done in 1949 before coming to the United States, just not to be too idle, and I had a lot of success with it. And later on I developed into doing other work, mostly copies from bigger pieces. But then I-- I did this here. I did this here, a scene of a shul, a famous painting. It's all done in charcoal. And I did this here. This here is a well-known depicting the type of persecutions Jewish people-- that the people went-- went-- this is before the Holocaust, before the Shoah. Depicts a pogrom, naturally, when Jews are save-- trying to save the Torahs. And you see the masses being uprooted, just like the story in the where the people had to leave Anatevka. Picture this. Picture Anatevka here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- I have done these things. And I have a few other major things upstairs, but it's no sense me going through the whole thing. I have one hanging right there, which is a relief work like this one here, and I was very proud of it. That-- that was my hobby-- parts. These were the hobbies. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: These were the hobbies that I indulged with. As a matter of fact, they gave us pieces, tiles-- this was linoleum tile, and they were about this big. So this is done of two pieces pasted together. The picture I need it for was the size-- this size, the enlargement. I enlarged this here from a postal card. I got a hold of a postal card in Germany, and I thought that would make a nice relief work. So I didn't have this size of linoleum, so I got two pieces, and you can see right here this is one piece and this is the other piece. And yet it came out very impressive, and I'm very proud of it. Not until this morning I realized exactly the date I was in Dachau, and it's right here. This is a certificate issued after the liberation by the International Information Office at Dachau, stating my name and a time I have been in Dachau-- April 13, 1945. This was before shipping us out to the Tyrol Mountains. This is one thing. And here's a document from Auschwitz about my mother. I have seen a notice in the paper about six, seven years ago that anybody who wants to make inquiries into Auschwitz about lost relatives can do so. It took about a year and a half or so. And I got a answer telling me that my father and my sister they have no records of, but they have records of my mother that have-- has arrived in Auschwitz. She has arrived in-- in the summer of 1943. She has arrived in the summer of '43, and she was gassed on November the 19th, 1943 with a group of 394 other prisoners in 1990-- 1943. This is a translation of the same document here. This is the original document. Here's a translation. And when I got this letter, I could not talk for a couple days. I keep this letter in a certain drawer, and every time I go to this drawer it is like you go to a cemetery and you touch the grave. That's how this letter-- that's what this letter means to me. My brother, I did not want to tell him. He was very, very sick at the time, and it was near a holiday. We were together. It was Thanksgiving. And my nephew, his son has somehow let out the secret that I got a letter from Auschwitz, and he insisted I show him. I refused to show it to him. When I show him this letter, it was just impossible. I thought it's going to really harm him. He broke down, and he was up in pieces. This letter is telling us that an interesting thing is that the date of my mother's birthday is 10 years younger. There's no question that this is my mother's name here, but they used to make themselves younger, women especially, trying to escape. So she made herself 10 years younger. My mother was born in 19-- in 1894, and here it gives the born date of 1905. And it-- the wording here is the English translation is 'The museum has documentation pertaining to a female prisoner, Kokotek Pesl, born in 1905 in Bedzin. She was brought to the concentration camp Oswiecim-Auschwitz-Brzezinka-Birkenau on 1/8/19-- 1943 with a transport from the ghetto Sosnowiec'-- which was a little town adjacent to ours. 'In the camp, marked female prisoner number 51624. In the day of 19th of November, 1943, together with a group of 394 Jewish females, she was gassed. Additional information of the above mentioned is not available in the museum. This information is based on the manifests of the incoming transports to the concentration camp Auschwitz-Brzezinka, Auschwitz-Birkenau, [GERMAN] Auschwitz--' and so and so. This is a document that we really hold dear, and we naturally observe the date of November the 19th as a day of anniversary.
